During an appointment with her primary care physician, a 28-year-old woman reports that she is pregnant. Your assistance in interpreting her thyroid hormone levels is requested. She has regular menstrual cycles every 28 to 30 days, and her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago. A home urine test kit for pregnancy has confirmed a positive result. She delivered a healthy baby boy 30 months ago following an uncomplicated pregnancy. Thirteen months ago, she had a miscarriage at 7 weeks' gestation. The patient reports tiredness that has been present for 3 months, but has worsened over the past 2 weeks. She has also developed mild nausea, mainly in the morning over the last week. She has not been able to take any�a to the medical records, it becomes apparent that the biochemical tests performed 7 weeks ago documented a serum TSH level of 3.8 mIU/L with a serum free T₄ level of 1.1 ng/dL (14.2 pmol/L). She reports a family history of rheumatoid arthritis and pernicious anemia in her maternal grandmother and Hashimoto thyroiditis in her mother.

On physical examination, weight is 128 lb (58.2 kg) (which has been stable) and height is 63 in (160 cm) (BMI = 22.7 kg/m²). Her pulse rate is 78 beats/min, and blood pressure is 126/72 mm Hg. She has a small palpa goiter, the outline of which is smooth. Physical examination findings are otherwise unremarkable. A pregnancy to done in the clinic is positive.

Thyroid function test results:

Serum TSH = 4.1 mIU/L

Serum free  $ T_{4}=1.0 $ ng/dL (12.9 pmol/L)

TPO antibodies = 32 IU/mL (32 kIU/L)

Which of the following should you recommend?
options: No therapy now; assessment of thyroid function again in 4 weeks
Desiccated thyroid extract containing both T_{4} and T_{3}
items.explanation:
## 10 ANSWER: C) BMI, normal; cardiovascular risk, increased

BMI determination (weight [kg]/height [m²]) has been the accepted criterion for the diagnosis of obesity. BMI values can identify persons at risk for weight-related medical complications and increased mortality. However,

BMI has limitations in that it does not provide information regarding body adiposity, which is well recognized contribute to pathophysiologic processes affecting health and mortality. This is particularly true for persons in normal-weight category (18.5-24.9 kg/m²) or overweight category (25.0-29.9 kg/m²). This patient's BMI of 23 kg/m² is considered normal (thus, Answers A, B, and E are incorrect). For patients with a BMI in this range, both composition can provide additional information regarding health risks. In individuals with an elevated BMI, those who have a healthy body composition (defined as a body fat percentage of less than 23.1% in men and less than 33.3% in women) may not have an increased risk for metabolic complications. This patient has a high body percentage (37%). Recent studies suggest that despite a normal BMI, increased adiposity confers an increased risk for metabolic complications and increased mortality, particularly for women. The state of having a normal BMI increased body fat has been termed normal-weight obesity. Romero-Corral et al reported that women with normal weight obesity have a 2.2-fold increased risk for cardiovascular mortality when compared with the risk of women with normal body adiposity (thus, Answers C is correct and Answers D is incorrect).

weight obesity have a 2.2-fold increase with normal body adiposity (thus, Answer C is correct and). There are several methods for measuring body composition. The most accurate tend to be cumbersome (underwater weighing) and not applicable to routine clinical practice. Anthropometric measurements (height, weight, waist circumference, skin-fold thickness) are easily assessed and provide valuable information regarding a patient's health risks, but they have limitations. Waist circumference measurement requires a consistent measure of the level of the iliac crest. Waist circumference for accuracy: flexible measuring tape in the horizontal view at the level of the iliac crest. Waist circumference identifies individuals with abdominal adiposity, which confers the highest risk for metabolic complications. Measurement of skin-fold thickness is less accurate and is most often used as a research tool. DXA provides a 3-compartment model for body composition, but it can have substantial intraindividual variability and it is a Body plethysmography uses air displacement instead of water displacement to measure body fat. Measurement correlates well with underwater weighing and are accurate. Impedance measurements are widely available (weeks), but have limitations. Impedance determines body water content, and formulas have been developed to convert that measurement to body fat. Several studies suggest that these methods underestimate body fat (2% variability) when compared with fat measurement determined by DXA. Documenting how this patient had the percentage of body fat determined is important. If it was determined by body plethysmography, the measurement most likely accurate.

##### Educational Objective:

Describe health risks associated with increased body adiposity regardless of body mass index.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Determining body composition in adults

##### Reference(s):

Lee SY, Gallagher D. Assessment methods in human body composition. Curr Opin Clin Nutr Metab Care. 2008;11(5):566-572.

Ginde SR, Geliebter A, Rubiano F, et al. Air displacement plethysmography: validation in overweight and obese subjects. Obes Res. 2005;13(7):1232-1234

items.question:
A 69-year-old woman in whom stage III papillary thyroid cancer was diagnosed 15 years ago has just moved to your area and presents for an initial visit. Her initial therapy consisted of thyroidectomy and radioiodine administration. The pathology from her surgery had documented a 3-cm papillary cancer with involvement of left central and left lateral cervical nodes. She had been monitored with recombinant TSH-stimulated radioiodine scanning and thyroglobulin measurement on 2 occasions (1 and 3 years after her diagnosis). She has had periodic cervical ultrasonography that has not revealed any suspicious thyroid bed lesions or cervical lymph nodes. Review of her past laboratory testing shows that thyroglobulin has been consistently undetectable and her TSH concentration has been between 0.01 and 0.09 mIU/L.

She has no specific concerns except for difficulty sleeping characterized by early awakening and inability to get back to sleep. She has osteoporosis, but no other medical problems. She has been treated with a bisphosphonate for the past 3 years because her bone density was decreasing. Her most recent femoral neck T score was -2.9. Her only medications are levothyroxine, alendronate, calcium supplements, and vitamin D. Her levothyroxine dosage is 137 mcg daily (2.3 mcg/kg as a weight-based dosage).

On physical examination, height is 67 in (170.2 cm) and weight is 130 lb (59.1 kg) (BMI = 20.4 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. There is no palpable tissue in the thyroid bed and no cervical adenopathy. Heart rate is 90 beats/min. She has a fine tremor of her outstretched hands.

Laboratory test results:

TSH = 0.09 mIU/L

Free  $ T_{4} $ = 1.8 ng/dL (23.2 pmol/L)

Serum thyroglobulin = <0.1 ng/mL (<0.1  $ \mu $g/L)

Cervical ultrasonography shows absence of tissue in the thyroid bed and some small, benign-appearing lymph nodes.

Which of the following is the best management plan?
options: Reduce the levothyroxine dosage to 112 mcg daily
Reduce the levothyroxine dosage to 112 mcg daily and add liothyronine, 5 mcg daily
Continue the current levothyroxine dosage and add a $ \beta $-adrenergic blocker
Order a recombinant TSH-stimulated whole-body radioiodine scan and thyroglobulin measurement
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

A 34-year-old man presents to his primary care physician after noticing the rapid development of a neck mass and some difficulty swallowing because of neck tenderness. Both the swelling and the neck symptoms have developed over the past 2 months. His primary care physician thought the mass was actually the patient's thyroid gland and hence referred the patient to you for further evaluation.

His recent medical history is notable for Hashimoto hypothyroidism that was diagnosed after he began experience fatigue and cold sensitivity 8 years ago. He takes levothyroxine, 88 mcg daily.

On physical examination, weight is 190 lb (86.4 kg) and height is 70 in (177.8 cm) (BMI = 27.3 kg/m $ ^{2} $) palpate a firm, right-sided thyroid nodule that is approximately 4 cm. There is associated tracheal deviation smaller nodule is also present in the left lobe.

Laboratory test results:

Serum TSH (while on levothyroxine) = 8.2 mIU/L

TPO antibodies = 120 IU/mL (120 kIU/L)

Ultrasonography of the patient's thyroid shows bilateral nodules, with the largest nodule being 3.8 cm and hypoechoic in appearance. The specimen obtained by FNAB is composed entirely of lymphocytes. The spec used for flow cytometry has poor cellularity, but shows a polyclonal population of B and T lymphocytes.

Which of the following is the best next step in his care?
options: Core biopsy of the largest thyroid nodule with repeated flow cytometry

items.question:
You are asked to evaluate a 16-year-old boy who presents with pubertal delay. His medical history includes primary adrenal insufficiency that was diagnosed 6 years ago and has been treated with hydrocortisone (10 mg in the morning, 5 mg in the afternoon, and 5 mg at 6 PM) and fludrocortisone, 200 mcg daily. His growth has been steady at 1 to 2 in (2.5 to 5.1 cm) per year, although he has not had a growth spurt. He has not started to shave, but has noticed some growth of axillary and pubic hair.

On physical examination, height is 62.2 in (158 cm) and weight is 121 lb (55 kg) (BMI = 22.1 kg/m²). Genital and pubic hair are Tanner stage 2. Testicular volume is 3 mL bilaterally. He is not hyperpigmented. Blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg with no postural drop. Findings on neck, thyroid, cardiovascular, abdominal, respiratory, and neurologic examinations are normal. There is no evidence of vitiligo. Results from routine biochemistry are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Serum FSH = 0.5 mIU/mL (0.5 IU/L)

Serum LH = 0.9 mIU/mL (0.9 IU/L)

Serum prolactin = 15 ng/mL (0.7 nmol/L)

Serum testosterone, undetectable

Serum TSH = 1.25 mIU/L

which of the following conditions most likely accounts for this clinical presentation?
items.explanation:
## 32 ANSWER: E) Congenital adrenal hypoplasia

The combination of primary adrenal insufficiency and pubertal delay make congenital adrenal hypoplasia (Answer E) the most likely diagnosis. This is a rare disorder that occurs in approximately 1 in 12,500 live births and can present in 4 different ways:

1. Sporadic form, associated with pituitary hypoplasia

2. Autosomal recessive form

3. X-linked form, associated with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism

4. X-linked form, associated with glycerol kinase deficiency, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, and psychomotor retardation

This vignette illustrates an example of an X-linked form of congenital adrenal hypoplasia. Although congenital adrenal hypoplasia often presents in the neonatal period, the X-linked form may not present until in later childhood, adolescence, and even early adulthood. X-linked congenital adrenal hypoplasia is caused by deletion or mutations in the NROB1 gene (also known as DAX1), which is located on the short arm of the X chromosome. This part of the X chromosome also includes the glycerol kinase and Duchenne muscular dystrophy loci that may be deleted, resulting in more severe clinical consequences (although not in this clinical vignette).

The protein encoded by NR0B1, DAX1, is a nuclear receptor protein that is fundamental to adrenal and gonadal development and is expressed in the pituitary, hypothalamus, adrenals, and gonads. It acts as a dominant negative regulator of transcription of other nuclear receptors such as SF1 and is an antagonist of the protein encoded by SRY, a nuclear protein responsible for male sexual development. The disorder presents with adrenal insufficiency during the neonatal period in sporadic and autosomal recessive forms or later in life in the X-linked forms. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism develops at puberty and is due to a combination of gonadal, pituitary, Sertoli-cell defects that develop because of DAX1 deficiency.

In a retrospective review of 103 children with adrenal insufficiency, the following causes were reported: congenital adrenal hyperplasia, 73%; Addison disease, 13%; peroxisomal disorders, 5%; lysosomal acid lipase deficiency (Wolman disease), 3%; unknown cause, 6%; and adrenal hypoplasia, 1%. Thus, adrenal insufficiency childhood is most often due to classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

In this vignette, a diagnosis of classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21α-hydroxylase deficiency (Answer D) is incorrect because this always presents in the neonatal period rather than during adolescence. In addition, testosterone production should not be affected by 21α-hydroxylase deficiency. However, affected patients can develop primary hypogonadism due to development to testicular rest tissue. There is no evidence of this clinically in the vignette and the biochemistry suggests secondary gonadal failure. Classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia caused by 11β-hydroxylase deficiency (Answer C) is also incorrect because this would present in infancy with adrenal failure and mineralocorticoid hypertension. Testosterone production is unaffected.

Although autoimmune adrenal failure and gonadal failure (autoimmune polyglandular syndrome) (Answer A) can present in children, primary adrenal failure at a young age should make the endocrinologist suspicious of a more unusual underlying cause. In addition, primary gonadal failure would occur in autoimmune polyglandular syndrome and this would be associated with elevated gonadotropin levels, which this patient does not have.

Peroxisomal disorders (adrenoleukodystrophy [Answer B] and adrenomyeloneuropathy) are rare X-linked disorders of β-oxidation that result in accumulation of very long chain fatty acids in tissues. They can present with adrenal insufficiency alone or in combination with spastic paraparesis (in adrenomyeloneuropathy) or severe neurologic features including quadriparesis, blindness, and severe cognitive dysfunction (in adrenoleukodystrophy). items.question:
A 66-year-old woman is referred to you after her primary care physician documented an elevated TSH level at a routine visit. She has no specific concerns. Since retiring from her job a year ago, she has noticed an

improved quality of life with a regular exercise routine. She has managed to lose a little weight. She smokes 1 pack of cigarettes daily and has done so for 40 years. For personal reasons, she maintains a low-sodium diet, which is also low in iodine content.

On physical examination, weight is 182 lb (82.7 kg) and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 31.2 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 128/78 mm Hg. Her 20-g thyroid gland has no discrete nodules. Her physical examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Serum TSH = 6.5 mIU/L

TPO antibodies = 200 IU/mL (200 KIU/L)

Measurements 1 month later:

TSH = 7.0 mIU/L

Serum free  $ T_{4}=1.1 $ ng/dL (14.2 pmol/L)

She has no records of being screened for thyroid dysfunction in the past.

Which of the following factors confers the greatest future risk of her becoming overtly hypothyroid?
items.explanation:
## 37 ANSWER: C) Presence of TPO antibodies

37 Subclinical hypothyroidism is diagnosed when a patient has an elevated serum TSH value accompanied by normal thyroid hormone levels. The natural history of subclinical hypothyroidism is particularly affected by 2 factors: the magnitude of the serum TSH elevation and the presence or absence of TPO antibodies.

Regarding TSH elevation, the study showed that 52% of patients documented to have baseline serum TSH values that were mildly elevated (5.0-9.9 mIU/L) had normal TSH values at the end of a 3-year period. The percentage of baseline TSH values in the ranges of 10.0 to 14.9 mIU/L and 15.0 to 19.9 mIU/L that normalized after 3 years in the same study were 13% and 5%, respectively. These observations have been confirmed in other studies. For example, in a study of patients older than 65 years, resolution of subclinical hypothyroidism occurred at the 2-year follow-up in 46% of those with TSH values between 4.5 and 6.9 mIU/L, in 10% of those with values between 7.0 and 9.9 mIU/L, and in 7% of those with values of 10.0 mIU/L or greater.

Nevertheless, there is an increased risk of developing overt hypothyroidism even with successively increasing baseline TSH values that fall within the normal range. This risk is affected by TPO antibody status. A study examined the risk of developing overt hypothyroidism during a longer follow-up period of 13 years according to initial TSH groupings of less than 2.5 mIU/L, 2.5 to 4.0 mIU/L, and greater than 4.0 mIU/L in patients with positive thyroid antibodies. Compared with an odds ratio of 1.4 in the group with TSH values less than 2.5 mIU/L, the odds ratio for hypothyroidism was 10 in the group with TSH values between 2.5 and 4.0 mIU/L and 108 for the group with TSH values greater than 4.0 mIU/L. The odds ratios were lower (1, 1.8, and 50, respectively) for the same TSH groups in patients without thyroid antibodies. Thus, some patients with mild TSH elevation, such as this patient, progress to overt hypothyroidism over prolonged periods and monitoring of thyroid function is indicated. A prospective study showed that the calculated risk ratios of overt hypothyroidism in the 3 TSH categories of less than 6.0 mIU/L, 6.0 to 12.0 mIU/L, and greater than 12.0 mIU/L were 1.2, 6.4, and 11.5, respectively, over 10 years of follow-up. These risk ratios increased to 5.3, 10.5, and 15.6 in patients with markedly elevated TPO antibody titers. Similarly, in one of the aforementioned studies, progression to overt hypothyroidism occurred with greater frequency in those with positive thyroid autoantibodies. Conversely, another study demonstrated that resolution of subclinical hypothyroidism is less likely in patients older than 65 years who have positive TPO antibodies than in those with negative antibodies (15% vs 48% of patients) (thus, Answer C is correct). The results of such studies have led to the recommendation to consider measurement of TPO antibodies as part of the evaluation of patients who have subclinical hypothyroidism.

However, in all these studies, patient age did not appear to predict progression to overt hypothyroidism (thus, Answer B is incorrect). Patient sex does not affect the likelihood of progression in some studies, although other studies do find a greater risk of progression in women. The reason for these discrepant findings with respect to sex may relate to the association between female sex and TPO antibodies, such that they are not independent factors. In the studies that have examined BMI, this factor did not affect the risk of developing overt hypothyroidism (thus, Answer E is incorrect). In some studies, the presence of a goiter increases the risk of future hypothyroidism. In one study of populations with different iodine intakes, a high-iodine intake is a further risk factor for development of overt hypothyroidism in patients with TPO antibodies (thus, Answer D is incorrect). items.question:
A 37-year-old woman presents for ongoing management of hyperthyroidism. Graves disease was diagnosed 3 months ago when she presented with typical symptoms and signs of thyrotoxicosis. She did not report any symptoms of ophthalmopathy and there was no clinical evidence of thyroid eye disease at presentation.

Laboratory test results at diagnosis:

Serum TSH = <0.01 mIU/L

TSH receptor antibodies, markedly elevated

Serum free  $ T_4 = 3.5 $ ng/dL (45.0 pmol/L)

TPO antibodies, markedly elevated

Serum free  $ T_{3}=9.8\ \text{pg/mL} $ (15.1 pmol/L)

Radioactive iodine uptake scan showed diffuse increased uptake consistent with a diagnosis of Graves disease. S was prescribed methimazole, 20 mg daily, and propranolol, 80 mg twice daily.

She smokes 10 cigarettes daily (she started smoking at age 18 years).

Although she initially felt better after starting methimazole and propranolol, she reports nausea within 2 hours of taking methimazole and increasing joint pains and itchy skin in the past 2 months. Moreover, she has developed progressive redness and watering of her eyes over the past 4 weeks. Acquaintances have commented that her eyes are bulging, but she does not have any vision disturbance.

On physical examination, weight is 141 lb (64.1 kg) and height is 65 in (165 cm) (BMI = 23.5 kg/m²). Her blood pressure is 128/68 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 64 beats/min in a regular rhythm. A diffuse, smooth goiter is palpable in her neck. She does not have a rash. Examination of her eyes reveals bilateral lid retraction (<2 mm) and proptosis (1 mm of the left eye and 2 mm of the right eye). There is bilateral conjunctival injection and bilateral swelling and redness of the eyelids. Visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally, and there is no restriction of eye movements.

Laboratory test results today:

Serum TSH = <0.01 mIU/L

Serum free  $ T_{4}=2.6 $ ng/dL (33.5 pmol/L)

Serum free  $ T_3 = 5.9 $ pg/mL (9.1 pmol/L)

The patient reports that her sister was successfully treated with radioactive iodine for Graves disease 5 years earlier and she would like to undergo the same therapy.

In addition to smoking cessation, which of the following should you recommend for the subsequent management of her thyrotoxicosis?
options: Administer radioiodine with concurrent corticosteroid treatment
Continue methimazole with concurrent corticosteroid treatment
items.explanation:
43 ANSWER: B) Administer radioiodine with concurrent control with the rate ofனхey disease. This patient has developed symptoms and signs of Graves' ophthalmopathy, an inflammatory eye disease. In most cases, it occurs in patients that develop in the orbit in association with autoimmune thyroid disorders. In most cases, it occurs in patients with current or previous Graves' disease and may present during, before, or after presentation with thyrotoxicosis. Approximately half of patients with Graves' disease have symptoms and signs of ophthalmopathy and 5% have severe disease, sometimes sight-threatening. The natural history of the disease is one of rapid deterioration followed by gradual improvement towards the baseline. Graves' ophthalmopathy is assessed by evaluation of disease activity and severity. The activity is determined using the clinical activity score, which generates 1 point each of the following: pain in primary gaze; pain with eye movement; chemosis; eyelid swelling; eyelid erythema; conjunctival redness; caruncula swelling; and, over the past 3 months, decreased visual acuity, increased diplopia, and proptosis. The maximum score is 10. Graves' ophthalmopathy is considered "active" with a clinical activity score of 3 or higher. The severity is graded as mild, moderate-to-severe, and sight-threatening as agreed upon by a consensus statement of the European Group of Graves' Orbitopathy (EUGOGO). Both activity and severity must be considered in therapeutic decisions regarding treatment of eye disease itself, as well as the treatment of hyperthyroidism. On the basis of the provided information, this patient has mild active Graves' ophthalmopathy.

In most cases, it occurs in patients that develop in the orbit in association with autoimmune thyroid disorders. In most cases, it occurs in patients with current or previous Graves' disease and may present during, before, or after presentation with thyrotoxicosis. Approximately half of patients with Graves' disease have symptoms and signs of ophthalmopathy and 5% have severe disease, sometimes sight-threatening. The natural history of the disease is one of rapid deterioration followed by gradual improvement towards the baseline. Graves' ophthalmopathy is assessed by evaluation of disease activity and severity. The activity is determined using the clinical activity score, which generates 1 point each of the following: pain in primary gaze; pain with eye movement; chemosis; eyelid swelling; eyelid erythema; conjunctival redness; caruncula swelling; and, over the past 3 months, decreased visual acuity, increased diplopia, and proptosis. The maximum score is 10. Graves' ophthalmopathy is considered "active" with a clinical activity score of 3 or higher. The severity is graded as mild, moderate-to-severe, and sight-threatening as agreed upon by a consensus statement of the European Group of Graves' Orbitopathy (EUGOGO). Both activity and severity must be considered in therapeutic decisions regarding treatment of eye disease itself, as well as the treatment of hyperthyroidism. On the basis of the provided information, this patient has mild active Graves' ophthalmopathy.

A 33-year-old man returns for thyroid cancer follow-up. He underwent thyroidectomy and central lymph node dissection 2 years ago. He was found to have a 1.5-cm papillary thyroid cancer that invaded the perithyroidal soft tissue. Twenty-four of 30 central lymph nodes contained thyroid cancer metastases. He was treated with 150 mCi radioactive iodine (^{131}I). Subsequently, his suppressed thyroglobulin while taking levothyroxine was 0.2 to 0.4 ng/mL (0.2-0.4 μg/L). Last year, a recombinant TSH-stimulated thyroglobulin level was 2.2 ng/mL (2.2 μg/L). A diagnostic radioiodine whole-body scan performed simultaneously showed no iodin uptake. Monitoring of his neck and thyroid bed has not shown any evidence of residual or recurrent disease. The patient has no other medical problems and takes his levothyroxine regularly. He does not report any symptoms associated with his iatrogenic hyperthyroidism. On physical examination, he appears well. Height is 72 in (183 cm), and weight is 161 lb (73.2 kg) (BMI = 21.8 kg/m²). He has no tachycardia or tremor. There is no palpable tissue in the thyroid bed and no enlarged lymph nodes are detected by palpation.

A 34-year-old man is referred to you with a 6-week history of right-sided neck swelling. He reports no symptoms consistent with thyroid dysfunction or compression. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily. He has no family history of thyroid disorders, but he reports a history of colon cancer in his mother. On physical examination, height is 72.5 in (184.2 cm) and weight is 174 lb (79.1 kg) (BMI = 23.3 kg/m $ ^{2} $). His blood pressure is 126/72 mm Hg. On visual inspection, the right thyroid lobe is enlarged. A 3 × 3-cm, firm, right-sided neck mass is palpable. It moves with swallowing. The rest of the examination findings are normal. Laboratory test results: Serum TSH = 4.5 mIU/L Serum free $ T_{4} $ = 1.3 ng/dL (16.7 pmol/L) Thyroid ultrasonography is performed (see images).
FNAB is performed. The cytopathologist reports a very cellular specimen composed of sheets and groups of follicular cells with frequent and conspicuous microfollicles. Mild variation in nuclear size is observed and some nuclear grooves are seen, but there are no convincing papillary nuclear features. The specimen is reported as a follicular lesion of undetermined significance. follicular lesion of undetermined significance. The patient has researched the management of thyroid nodules on the Internet. He is concerned that thyroid surgery may result in recurrent laryngeal nerve damage, which could affect his voice. He has read that additional laboratory testing may be performed to provide further information regarding the prediction of malignancy in thyroid nodules. If you were to proceed with further molecular or laboratory testing, which of the following would most reliably exclude thyroid malignancy in this situation?
options: Mutation analysis panel combining point mutations in BRAF (Val600Glu) and rearrangements in RET/PTC and PAX8/PPARG
items.explanation:
## 53 ANSWER: B) Gene expression classifier Thyroid nodules occur in 20% to 75% of ac

53 Thyroid nodules occur in 20% to 75% of adults and 5% to 15% of these are proven to be malignant. FNAB is the most reliable clinical tool for determining whether a nodule is benign or malignant. However, approximately 10% to 25% of FNABs of thyroid nodules yield an indeterminate result. The Bethesda System for reporting thyroid cytopathology is used to classify these lesions as atypia of undetermined significance or follicular lesions of undetermined significance (AUS/FLUS—Bethesda Category III) or follicular neoplasm/suspicious for follicular neoplasm (FN/SFN—Bethesda Category IV). The reported risk of malignancy in these indeterminate lesions varies and is 7% to 48% for AUS/FLUS lesions and 20% to 30% for FN/SFN lesions. The Bethesda classification also labels lesions suspicious for malignancy as category V and the reported malignancy risk in these lesions is 50% to 75%. Recent advances in thyroid cancer biology have led to the development of several tests to assist in determining whether indeterminate nodules are benign or malignant. Currently, molecular tests to refine the diagnosis of thyroid malignancy are meant to complement and not to replace clinical judgment, sonographic assessment, and visual cytopathology interpretation.

Two main tests are currently marketed to assess for malignancy in thyroid nodules: gene expression classifiers (Answer B) and mutation analysis panels (Answer C). Gene expression classifiers are based on gene expression profiles of surgically proven benign and malignant thyroid nodules. They evaluate for the presence of a benign expression profile consisting of 142 mRNAs. Validation studies have shown high negative predictive values (94% to 95%) and sensitivity (90%) among nodules with the cytologic interpretation of AUS/FLUS or FN/SFN. Gene expression classifiers are therefore considered a “rule out” test to identify nodules that are benign. However, because a substantial number of benign nodules do not have a gene expression profile classified as benign, the test can therefore not always exclude malignancy. Moreover, clinical validation has not been performed for nodules smaller than 1 cm, nor has this form of testing been evaluated in the pediatric population. The use of this test has been reported to obviate the need for surgical excision in approximately 50% of cases. The specificity (52%) and positive predictive value (47%) of gene expression classifiers in nodules with indeterminate cytology are poor and their use is therefore not recommended to confirm the presence of malignancy. Preliminary cost analysis indicates that the use of gene expression classifiers may be cost-effective, although studies performed thus far have been largely based on preliminary data. Because the patient in this vignette has a lesion classified as FLUS (which carries a relatively low risk of malignancy), proceeding with a gene expression classifier test (Answer B) would be

the most appropriate of the listed options. The false-negative rate is 5% to 6%, so patients with a negative result should still be followed up closely with repeated ultrasonography.

BRAF Val600Glu and KAS point mutations, as well as common rearrangements of RET/PTC and PAX8/PPARG, are common drivers behind many thyroid cancers. It is estimated that 1 of these molecular parameters is present in 60% of well-differentiated thyroid cancer. The components of the mutation analysis panel are available as standalone tests or together as panels that assess for the presence of these molecular alterations simultaneously. Mutation analysis has high specificity and very good positive predictive value. However, because approximately 40% of malignant nodules with indeterminate cytology do not harbor any of these genetic mutations, they can reliably exclude malignancy (thus, Answers C and E are incorrect). They are considered good “rule-in” tests to confirm the presence

You are asked to evaluate a 43-year-old woman after thyroidectomy to determine the best next step in her care. She has a 10-year history of bilateral thyroid nodules that were initially identified when she felt fullness in her neck. Ultrasonography at that time showed multiple nodules, all smaller than 2.1 cm. FNAB was performed on the 2 largest nodules, and findings from cytologic analysis were benign. Her TSH level at the time was normal. She was followed up with annual examinations and had no further intervention. In the past 6 months, she has been concerned that the sensation of neck fullness is increasing and that the nodules are enlarging. Repeated ultrasonography verifies that nodules on both lobes are larger. She undergoes elective total thyroidectomy. The pathologic findings for the larger nodules are benign, documenting nodular hyperplasia. There are no abnormal lymph nodes noted on inspection, and findings on histologic examination of 2 nodes included in the surgical specimen are negative. However, 6 abnormal foci of cells are also identified. The largest of the lesions is 4.5 mm in diameter. Two of these are shown in the image on the left (black arrows). A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of a sore throat, general malaise, anorexia, and a high fever associated with rigors. Severe Graves hyperthyroidism was diagnosed months earlier, and she has since been treated with methimazole, 30 mg daily, and propranolol, 80 mg twice daily. She had lost 15.5 lb (7 kg) before Graves hyperthyroidism was diagnosed, and she has regained 2.2 lb (1 kg) since starting methimazole therapy.

On physical examination she is an ill-appearing woman. Weight is 138.5 lb (63 kg) and height is 66 in (167.6 cm) (BMI = 22.4 kg/m²). Her temperature is 102.9°F (39.4°C), pulse rate is 80 beats/min, and blood pressure is 124/76 mm Hg. There is a white exudate overlying the tonsils and a small mouth ulcer. She has signs of mild Graves' ophthalmopathy, and a moderately enlarged, soft, diffuse goiter is palpable in her neck. Findings on systems examination are otherwise unremarkable.

Urgent laboratory evaluation is performed:

Serum TSH = <0.01 mIU/L

## 80 ANSWER: E) TSH-secreting adenoma

The unifying diagnosis that would explain all the data presented in this vignette is development of a TSH-secreting adenoma (Answer E) superimposed on a preexisting diagnosis of Hashimoto hypothyroidism. The patient's serum TSH concentrations have been increasing despite an escalation in her levothyroxine dosage, thus suggesting autonomous TSH production. The headaches that the patient is experiencing, along with the recent motor vehicle crashes perhaps related to altered peripheral vision (bitemporal hemianopsia), could indicate a space-occupying lesion within the sella turcica. In a series of 25 patients with TSH-secreting adenomas followed up at the National Institutes of Health, headaches and visual field deficits occurred in 39% and 37% of patients, respectively. A pituitary MRI would be the next step to determine whether this patient does, in fact, have a pituitary adenoma. A visual field examination would determine whether such a lesion, if present, was associated with visual field defects.

TSH-secreting tumors usually present in a hyperthyroid patient who has an inappropriately normal or elevated TSH level in the face of an elevated free T $ _{4} $ level. In some instances, the patient may have previously undergone

therapy for hyperthyroidism. The patient presented here had a preexisting diagnosis of Hashimoto hypothyroidism. Presumably, her functional pituitary tumor gradually manifested over the years as a trend for needing higher levothyroxine dosages intended to return her serum TSH value to the reference range. It is the consequence of increasing levothyroxine replacement that has generated her hyperthyroidism, not the endogenous thyroid hormone production as described earlier, as presumably the patient has minimal functional thyroid tissue as a result of her Hashimoto disease.

Anomalous or discordant results from thyroid function studies have a wide differential diagnosis that includes assay problems, interfering drugs, regimen adherence issues, TSH-secreting tumors, and resistance to thyroid hormone. Both TSH and free T₄ concentrations in the upper part of the reference range can be seen when a patient resumes levothyroxine therapy, possibly with addition of “make-up” doses after a period of abstinence. However, this is not the best answer in this vignette because her free T₄ levels do track appropriately with the changing levothyroxine dosage. Moreover, patients who intermittently take thyroid hormone replacement would not manifest constant hyperthyroidism (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Similarly, altered absorption of levothyroxine (Answer B) is incorrect because her free T₄ concentration has increased beyond the upper end of the reference range.

is incorrect because Prituitary resistance to thyroid hormone (Answer C) is a rare disorder. Seventy-five percent of these cases are dominantly inherited, such that an abnormal pattern of thyroid function is often seen in first-degree relatives. The onset of abnormal thyroid function would not occur in adulthood, as it has in this patient. Potential means of distinguishing between a TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma and resistance to thyroid hormone include documenting a high pituitary glycoprotein hormone α-subunit level, a high sex hormone-binding globulin level, and an attenuated response to thyrotropin-releasing hormone injection—findings that are characteristic of TSH-secreting tumors. Certain antibodies such as heterophilic antibodies or human anti-animal antibodies can interfere with immunometric 2-site TSH assays and give a falsely elevated TSH value. Normally, TSH binds to both a capture antibody and a detection antibody, thus generating a true signal. These interfering antibodies similarly bind both the capture and detection antibodies, generating a false signal and artificially elevating the TSH reading. You are asked to assist in the evaluation and management of a 9-year-old boy who has experienced anorexia and weight loss over the past few weeks. Before this, he had been eating and growing normally. He was born at full term with a normal birth weight. His medical history includes attention deficit hyperactivity disorder diagnosed 1 year ago. There is no family history of adrenal disease in any first- or second-degree relatives.

On physical examination, height is 50 in (127 cm) and weight is 57.2 lb (26 kg) (BMI = 16.1 kg/m²). You observe areas of pigmentation over his palmar creases. Blood pressure is 102/68 mm Hg (supine) and 92/64 mm Hg (standing). Neurologic examination reveals hyperreflexia in both lower limbs, bilateral clonus, and upward plantar reflex. Tanner staging is prepubertal.

Laboratory test results:

 $$ \mathrm{Sodium}=133\mathrm{mEq/L}(133\mathrm{mmol/L}) $$ 

Cortisol (8 AM) = 1.2  $ \mu $g/dL (33.1 nmol/L)

 $$ \mathrm{P o t a s s i u m}=5.6\mathrm{m E q/L}(5.6\mathrm{m o m o l/L}) $$ 

ACTH (8 AM) = 210 pg/mL (46.2 pmol/L)

 $$ Creatinine=0.7\ mg/dL(61.9\ \mu mol/L) $$ 

Adrenal autoantibodies, negative

A mutation in which of the following genes and its associated clinical syndrome most likely accounts for this boy's clinical presentation?
options: NR0B1 (also known as DAXI) (congenital adrenal hypoplasia)
items.explanation:
## 84 ANSWER: A) ABCD1 (adrenoleukodystrophy) This

This child presents with primary adrenal insufficiency. Statistically, the most likely cause of primary adrenal failure in children is classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which can be due to mutations in the CYP21A2 gene (most common) (Answer D) or the HSD3B2 gene (Answer E), which encode the enzymes 21α-hydroxylase and 3β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase, respectively. However, classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to these enzyme defects presents in the neonatal period with adrenal insufficiency and salt wasting, as well as ambiguous genitalia in girls. Therefore, given that this child did not present with adrenal failure until age 9 years, classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia is an incorrect diagnosis. Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia can present with hyperandrogenism at the time of puberty, or shortly thereafter, but does not present with adrenal insufficiency.

The combination of primary adrenal insufficiency in a child with a behavioral disorder and mildly abnormal neurologic findings makes adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD) the most likely underlying cause. ALD is an X-linked disorder caused by mutations in the ABCD1 gene (Answer A). Mutations in ABCD1 prevent transport of very long chain fatty acids into peroxisomes, thus inhibiting their oxidation and breakdown. The subsequent accumulation of very long chain fatty acids in affected organs (CNS, Leydig cells of the testes, and adrenal cortex) results in the various clinical presentations of ALD, which can be categorized into 3 main forms:

#### Childhood Cerebral Forms (35%)

Childhood cerebral forms of ALD typically present in children between the ages of 4 and 8 years. The initial neurologic manifestations are learning disabilities and behavioral disorders that may be labeled as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, as was the case in this vignette. Subsequent neurologic deterioration ensues (cognitive impairment, blindness, quadriparesis), and death occurs within 5 to 10 years of diagnosis. Most affected persons also have primary adrenal insufficiency.

#### Adrenomyeloneuropathy (40%-45%)

adrenomyeloneuropathy usually presents in adulthood with a peak age of onset of 20 to 40 years. The neurologic abnormalities seen in adrenomyeloneuropathy are less severe than those in the childhood cerebral forms of ALD and typically consist of spinal cord dysfunction with eventual spastic paraparesis. Gonadal and adrenal dysfunction also typical.

#### Isolated Adrenal Insufficiency (10%)

drenal insufficiency occurs in isolation in up to 10% of ALD cases. Adrenal insufficiency usually presents at a young age (before age 7.5 years), but it can occasionally present in adulthood. Therefore, boys who present with parent Addison disease in early childhood should be evaluated for ALD.

LD is diagnosed by measuring very long chain fatty acid levels in either blood or fibroblasts. These are elevated nearly all males with the disorder and in 85% of affected females (although females with an  $ ABCD1 $ mutation are carriers and are usually not seriously affected). In most affected male patients, the combination of typical clinical features and markedly elevated very long chain fatty acid levels is sufficient to establish the diagnosis of -linked ALD. To confirm the clinical diagnosis and to facilitate genetic counseling, molecular genetic testing of  $ ABCD1 $ gene may be performed. Genetic testing is particularly important in patients with borderline very long chain fatty acid levels or atypical features, as well as in female patients.

Mutations in the NR0B1 gene (also known as DAXI) (Answer B) and the SF1 gene (Answer C) are associated with congenital adrenal hypoplasia. This is also a rare X-linked disorder that presents with adrenal insufficiency but no neurologic or behavioral findings. Affected patients develop hypogonadotropic hypogonadism that is evident at puberty. Thus, the presence of apparent attention deficit

A 72-year-old man is referred to you by his primary care physician after the detection of a retrosternal goiter with tracheal deviation on a chest radiograph. The patient has a 6-month history of mild positional dyspnea at rest and intermittent orthopnea. On direct questioning, he reports no symptoms of stridor, dysphagia, or dysphoria. He has a longstanding history of mild chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and is an ex-smoker. On physical examination, weight is 185 lb (84.1 kg) (which has been stable), and height is 72 in (182.9 cm) (BMI = 25.1 kg/m²). His blood pressure is 142/76 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 72 beats/min and regular. He has a multinodular goiter in the neck that is palpable in a neutral position and visible in a supine position with neck extension. The lower lobe of the thyroid gland extends below the sternal notch and cannot be palpated when the patient is lying in the supine position with mild neck extension or with swallowing. The Pemberton sign is negative. There is tracheal deviation to the left. Examination of his chest reveals a mild end expiratory wheeze over the peripheral lung fields, but is otherwise unremarkable. The rest of his examination findings are normal. Flow volume loop does not show evidence of clinically significant extrathoracic obstruction. Laboratory test results: Serum TSH = 3.5 mIU/L Serum free T₄ = 1.2 ng/dL (15.4 pmol/L) TPO antibodies = 18 IU/mL (18 kIU/L) Review of the chest radiograph ordered by the primary care physician confirms the presence of a retrosternal goite with tracheal deviation to the left. A CT of his neck and chest is performed (see images).
Axial view.
Coronal view. The patient's primary care physician has suggested that he will require surgery, but the patient would like to avoid surgery if possible. Which of the following treatment strategies should you recommend now?
options: Prednisone, 40 mg daily for 2 weeks, then taper over 1 month
Levothyroxine sufficient to suppress TSH below the lower limit of the reference range
No therapy now; perform another CT in 4 to 6 months
Radioactive iodine ablation following administration of recombinant human TSH

A 39-year-old woman with Hashimoto hypothyroidism returns for follow-up after adjustment of the levothyroxine dosage. Hypothyroidism was diagnosed at age 31 years in the postpartum period. She had a high titer of TPO antibodies and did not recover normal thyroid function. She ultimately developed a serum TSH concentration of 70 mIU/L before levothyroxine was prescribed at a dosage of 112 mcg daily. Her levothyroxine dosage was gradually increased to 150 mcg daily. She had been clinically and biochemically euthyroid while taking this dosage for several years, with her serum TSH concentration being between 1.0 and 3.0 mIU/L.

Over the past 6 months, she has unintentionally lost 8.8 lb (4 kg). She describes being more irritable, having modest heat intolerance, and having a diminished exercise capacity. You have had her return 3 times in the last 6 months for thyroid hormone testing and on each occasion her TSH was suppressed. Thus, her levothyroxine dosage was reduced to 50 mcg daily (her current dosage).

On physical examination, height is 65.5 in (166.4 cm) and weight is 153 lb (69.5 kg) (BMI = 25.1 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 124/74 mm Hg, and heart rate is 88 beats/min. Her thyroid gland is slightly enlarged and has an irregular texture. She has brisk patellar reflexes.

Thyroid function test results:

Serum TSH = 0.01 mIU/L

Free  $ T_{4} $ = 2.1 ng/dL (27.0 pmol/L)

She confirms that levothyroxine is her only prescription medication. She never takes extra tablets. You inspect the tablets in her levothyroxine pill container and find no error in the prescription. She takes no over-the-counter supplements. Your review of her history does not uncover any reason accounting for her decreased levothyroxine requirement.

Over several subsequent visits, and after further levothyroxine dosage adjustments, you discontinue her levothyroxine. Thyroid function testing 6 weeks later shows a TSH concentration of 2.1 mIU/L and a free T₄ concentration of 1.4 ng/dL (18.0 pmol/L). With this last blood test, you also measure a serum total T₃ level, a serum thyroglobulin concentration, and a spot urinary iodine concentration. Her total T₃ level is 120 ng/dL (1.8 nmol/L), her thyroglobulin concentration is 43 ng/mL (43 μg/L), and her random urinary iodine excretion is 177 μg/L (reference range, 28-544 μg/L).

#### Which of the following best explains euthyroidism after the discontinuation of levothyroxine in this patient?
options: Ingestion of an over-the-counter supplement containing tiratricol
Ingestion of large amounts of nutritional supplements containing iodine
Autoimmune thyroid disease with current predominance of thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies
Surreptitious ingestion of desiccated porcine thyroid extract
items.explanation:
## 90 ANSWER: D) Autoimmune thyroid disease with current predominance of thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies

It is certainly unusual for a patient who had been euthyroid while maintained on a midrange levothyroxine dosage to be capable of maintaining a euthyroid state off any levothyroxine. However, the patient's initial TSH and TPO antibody elevation, combined with a levothyroxine requirement for 8 years, lends support to the correctness of her original diagnosis. A shift from hypothyroidism to hyperthyroidism with transition to a euthyroid state in between, although rare, has been described in the literature. This may be caused by fluctuating concentrations of thyrotropin receptor-blocking antibodies and thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies, such that a euthyroid state occurs when the 2 antibodies are in balance, and a hyperthyroid phase is seen when thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies predominate. Thyrotropin receptor-blocking antibodies, however, were not measured in this patient when Hashimoto hypothyroidism was first diagnosed. In addition, a shift between hypothyroidism, euthyroidism, and hyperthyroidism associated with alteration of thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibody concentration or activity in persons with TPO antibodies has also been reported. This may be the explanation for the sequence of events described in the vignette, with a patient who was euthyroid on levothyroxine replacement now maintaining euthyroidism without levothyroxine (Answer D). This scenario would also explain why the patient had a slightly enlarged thyroid gland despite having been treated for hypothyroidism for 8 years.

A 2008 survey suggests that use of over-the-counter medications and dietary supplements is quite common. In this survey, 46% of those taking prescription medications also used over-the-counter medications, while 52% were concurrently taking dietary supplements. Dietary supplements or nutraceuticals that are marketed as enhancing thyroid function may contain iodine, tyrosine, thyroid hormone extract, or thyroid hormone analogues. Given these types of statistics, it is wise for the physician to inquire whether a patient is taking supplements of any kind, particularly because interactions between these supplements and prescribed medications can be hazardous. In addition, if clinical findings or laboratory results do not make sense, it is reasonable to investigate the possibility of supplement use.

Liothyronine (Answer A) is a prescription medication that is not used as standard or care because of its short half-life and the difficulty in avoiding intermittent thyrotoxicosis because of the peaks associated with its administration. If this patient were taking liothyronine at the time of her last laboratory test, her total T₃ level would be expected to be higher, while her free T₄ level would be expected to be lower. Therefore, liothyronine is unlikely to be the explanation. A similar disproportionate T₃ thyrotoxicosis would also be expected to be associated with thyroid extract (Answer E). Tiratricol (Answer B) is a metabolite of T₄ with intrinsic thymomimetic activity that has been used for “thyroid stimulation” and weight loss. The US Food and Drug Administration has banned products containing tiratricol, but they can still be obtained through various sources. However, if this patient were taking tiratricol alone at the time of her last laboratory assessment, her free T₄ concentration would be expected to be lower. The recommended daily allowance of iodine is 150 mcg. Large iodine doses are sometimes taken by patients in an attempt to stimulate thyroid function, and these excessive doses can lead to either hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Hypothyroidism typically ensues if the patient has underlying Hashimoto thyroiditis, whereas hyperthyroidism may be precipitated in individuals with underlying thyroid autonomy such as Graves disease. It

would appear unlikely that this patient's current thyroid t

A shift from hypothyroidism to hyperthyroidism with transition to a euthyroid state in between, although rare, has been described in the literature. This may be caused by fluctuating concentrations of thyrotropin receptor-blocking antibodies and thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies, such that a euthyroid state occurs when the 2 antibodies are in balance, and a hyperthyroid phase is seen when thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibodies predominate. Thyrotropin receptor-blocking antibodies, however, were not measured in this patient when Hashimoto hypothyroidism was first diagnosed. In addition, a shift between hypothyroidism, euthyroidism, and hyperthyroidism associated with alteration of thyrotropin receptor-stimulating antibody concentration or activity in persons with TPO antibodies has also been reported. This may be the explanation for the sequence of events described in the vignette, with a patient who was euthyroid on levothyroxine replacement now maintaining euthyroidism without levothyroxine (Answer D). This scenario would also explain why the patient had a slightly enlarged thyroid gland despite having been treated for hypothyroidism for 8 years.

A 2008 survey suggests that use of over-the-counter medications and dietary supplements is quite common. In this survey, 46% of those taking prescription medications also used over-the-counter medications, while 52% were concurrently taking dietary supplements. Dietary supplements or nutraceuticals that are marketed as enhancing thyroid function may contain iodine, tyrosine, thyroid hormone extract, or thyroid hormone analogues. Given these types of statistics, it is wise for the physician to inquire whether a patient is taking supplements of any kind, particularly because interactions between these supplements and prescribed medications can be hazardous. In addition, if clinical findings or laboratory results do not make sense, it is reasonable to investigate the possibility of supplement use.

items.question:
A 25-year-old woman presents for further evaluation and management of a newly discovered thyroid nodule. During a visit for an annual examination, her gynecologist palpated a left-sided thyroid nodule. The gynecologist had ordered thyroid function testing and thyroid ultrasonography and referred her to you. The patient has no history of thyroid dysfunction. She has no symptoms suggestive of either hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism and no compressive symptoms. She has no medical problems. Her only medication is oral contracentives. On physical examination, height is 66 in (167.6 cm) and weight is 136.5 lb (62 kg) (BMI = 22 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 124/68 mm Hg. On visual inspection, you note that her thyroid gland is asymmetric, with the left lobe being larger than the right lobe. You palpate a mobile, soft, 2-cm nodule within the left lobe of the thyroid. Otherwise, she has no abnormal examination findings.
Thyroid function test results: $$ TSH=1.2mIU/L $$ $$ \mathrm{Free~T}_{4}=1.3\mathrm{~ng/dL}(16.7\mathrm{~pmol/L}) $$ You review her ultrasonography, which shows that the 2.2-cm nodule palpated on the left side of her thyroid is purely cystic. However, the ultrasound images also show a smaller 1.2-cm, nonpalpable nodule in the contralateral lobe (see image, arrow). Which of the following is the best next diagnostic step?
options: FNAB of both the 2.2-cm and 1.2-cm nodules
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

items.question:
A 52-year-old man attends an urgently scheduled appointment with a cardiologist. He reports increasing symptoms of exertional angina over the past 4 weeks. He has no medical history of cardiovascular disease. He has bipolar disorder for which he takes lithium regularly. He smokes 40 cigarettes daily and has done so for more than 30 years. He reports a family history of hypothyroidism in both his mother and a maternal aunt.

On physical examination, weight is 202 lb (91.8 kg) and height is 69 in (175.3 cm) (BMI = 29.8 kg/m²). His pulse rate is 94 beats/min and regular, and blood pressure is 152/85 mm Hg. He has a moderately sized multinodular goiter. Other examination findings are unremarkable. His electrocardiogram indicates ischemic changes in the anterior leads, but does not show evidence of an acute coronary event.

Laboratory test results:

Tropinin I = <0.6 ng/mL (<0.6  $ \mu $g/L)

Serum TSH = 0.07 mIU/L

Creatinine kinase = 163 U/L (2.7  $ \mu $kat/L)

Serum free  $ T_{4} $ = 1.6 ng/dL (20.6 pmol/L)

The cardiologist wishes to perform a coronary angiogram and asks for your advice regarding the risk and potential management of thyroid dysfunction caused by iodinated contrast medium in this patient.

How should you advise the cardiologist?
options: Proceed with the angiogram; no additional treatment required
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

items.question:
You are contacted by a colleague in obstetrics to provide a consult on a 34-year-old woman who is 10 weeks +2 days pregnant. She has been admitted to the antenatal ward with severe nausea and vomiting. The patient gives a 3-week history of increasing nausea and vomiting, muscle weakness, increasing palpitation, tiredness, and weight loss of 6.5 lb (3 kg). She has not noticed neck swelling and she has no symptoms of sore and gritty eyes or vision disturbance.

Her medical history includes asthma diagnosed in childhood and managed with a beclomethasone inhaler twice daily and a salbutamol inhaler when required. Her asthma has now been stable for several years, and she has not been hospitalized for an acute attack for 5 years. She has a family history of autoimmune hypothyroidism in her grandmother and type 1 diabetes mellitus in her younger brother.

On physical examination, weight is 186 lb (84.5 kg) and height is 67 in (170.2 cm) (BMI = 29.1 kg/m²). Her blood pressure is 115/62 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 110 beats/min in a regular rhythm. There is a mild fine tremor of the outstretched hands. She does not have a clinically palpable goiter, there is no thyroid bruit, and there are no signs of thyroid eye disease. The rest of her examination findings are unremarkable. Pelvic ultrasonography has confirmed the presence of an intrauterine singleton pregnancy.

Laboratory test results:

Serum TSH = <0.01 mIU/L

Serum free  $ T_{4} $ = 3.3 ng/dL (42.5 pmol/L)

Serum free T_{3}=5.8 pg/mL (8.9 pmol/L)

Creatinine = 1.1 mg/dL (97.2 μmol/L)

Serum sodium = 138 mEq/L (138 mmol/L)

Potassium = 3.1 mEq/L (3.1 mmol/L)

Serum urea nitrogen = 30 mg/dL (10.7 mmol/L)

Blood glucose = 90 mg/dL (5.00 mmol/L)

 $ \beta $-hCG = 180,000 mIU/mL (180,000 IU/L)

Liver function, normal

Dipstick analysis of urine, presence of ketones

(+++) but negative for glucose, blood, and protein

Results of thyroid function testing before pregnancy, including assessment for TPO antibodies, were normal.

Since being admitted to the hospital, the patient has been started on intravenous rehydration with isotonic saline with 40 mmol potassium chloride and is receiving regular intravenous metoclopramide. She continues to nauseate and has vomited twice this morning.

In addition to continuing supportive treatment, which of the following is the best next step in the patient's care?
items.explanation:
## 97 ANSWER: E) No antithyroid treatment is indicated The i

The interpretation of thyroid function test results in pregnancy can be challenging because serum TSH concentrations fall in early pregnancy as a physiologic response to the stimulating effects of hCG on the TSH receptor with peak hCG concentrations between 7 and 11 weeks' gestation. Most studies also report a substantial decrease in serum free  $ T_{4} $ concentrations with the progression of gestation. Serum free  $ T_{4} $ measurements in pregnant women are complicated by increased thyroxine-binding globulin and decreased albumin concentrations, which result in marked interassay and interindividual variability when immune-mediated assays are used. Hence total  $ T_{4} $ measurement by extraction/liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry has been recommended, but this is not widely available. Many centers have now adopted the use of gestation-specific reference ranges that are assay and population dependent. In the absence of assay-specific ranges, the Endocrine Society and the American Thyroid Association have recommended TSH reference ranges of 0.1 to 2.5 mIU/L, 0.2 to 3.0 mIU/L, and 0.3 to 3.0 mIU/L in the first, second, and third trimesters, respectively.

During pregnancy, any subnormal TSH value should be evaluated in conjunction with serum free  $ T_{4} $. The diagnosis of clinical hyperthyroidism is confirmed in the presence of suppressed or undetectable serum TSH and elevated serum free  $ T_{4} $. Untreated hyperthyroidism can have severe consequences for the pregnant woman, pregnancy outcome, and the fetus. In the presence of an undetectable or very low serum TSH and elevated serum free  $ T_{4} $, the differential diagnosis in most cases is between Graves hyperthyroidism and gestational transient thyrotoxicosis. The central element in Graves disease is autoimmunity to the TSH receptor, and the hyperthyroidism is caused by TSH receptor-stimulating antibodies. In gestational transient thyrotoxicosis, the thyrotoxicosis is due to high concentrations of hCG stimulating the TSH receptor because of structural homology between hCG and TSH molecules. In Graves disease, thyroid production of  $ T_{3} $ is high and serum total  $ T_{3} $ or serum free  $ T_{3} $ concentrations are typically more elevated than serum total  $ T_{4} $ or free  $ T_{4} $ concentrations. Gestational transient thyrotoxicosis is usually associated with  $ T_{4} $ thyrotoxicosis that can be diagnosed by documenting serum total  $ T_{4} $ or free  $ T_{4} $ levels above the upper normal limit, but with only slightly raised or normal serum total  $ T_{3} $ or free  $ T_{3} $ concentrations.

A careful history and clinical examination are of utmost importance in establishing the etiology of thyrotoxicosis. In both situations, common clinical manifestations include palpitation, anxiety, hand tremor, and

heat intolerance. Factors suggestive of gestational thyrotoxicosis include the absence of a personal history of thyroid disease and of a family history of autoimmune thyroid or other disorders, diagnosis in the first trimester of pregnancy, the absence of symptoms or signs before pregnancy, an association with hyperemesis, a relationship with multiple gestation, the absence of clinical signs of Graves disease (including goiter and thyroid-associated ophthalmopathy), and mild or absent clinical signs of thyrotoxicosis.

The management of gestational hyperthyroidism depends on symptom severity. In women with hyperemesis gravidarum, control of vomiting and treatment of dehydration with intravenous fluids compose the typical treatment. The patient in this vignette has hypokalemia and correction of electrolyte abnormalities is important. The course of gestational thyrotoxicosis is usually self-limiting and serum T₄ typically returns to normal by 14 to 18 weeks' gestation (thus, Answer E is correct). The obstetric outcome is not improved in isolated cases in which gestational thyrotoxicosis

items.question:
A 62-year-old woman is referred for evaluation of goiter. She was found to have asymmetric, right-side neck enlargement with a palpable $ 1.5 \times 2 $-cm thyroid nodule. Laboratory test results: Serum TSH = 4.2 mIU/L Serum free $ T_{4}=1.1 $ ng/dL (14.2 pmol/L) TPO antibodies, mildly elevated On physical examination, weight is 173 lb (78.6 kg) and height is 65 in (165 cm) (BMI = 28.8 kg/m²). Her blood pressure is 145/84 mm Hg. On direct questioning, she reports no symptoms of thyroid dysfunction or compression. You confirm the presence a right-sided multinodular goiter with a palpable thyroid nodule measuring 2 × 2.5 cm. Thyroid ultrasonography is performed. This confirms the presence of a right-sided multinodular goiter with 3 nodules measuring 20 × 16 × 26 mm, 10 × 14 × 11 mm, and 9 × 12 × 10 mm. There is no evidence of increased vascular flow on Doppler sonography in any of the nodules. There are no thyroid nodules on the left side and no enlarged or abnormal neck nodes. The images of the palpable nodule and 2 other right-sided nodules are displayed below.
(Palpable nodule, 20 × 16 × 26 mm) (Nodule 2, 10 × 14 × 11 mm)
(Nodule 3, 9 × 12 × 10 mm) Which of the following best describes the most appropriate management?
options: Perform FNAB of the palpable thyroid nodule
Perform FNAB of all 3 thyroid nodules
Measure serum thyroglobulin and thyroglobulin antibodies
Perform ultrasonography again in 6 months to determine interval growth
items.explanation:
## 113 ANSWER: A) Perform FNAB of the palpable thyroid nodule

113 AN Patients presenting with nodular goiter should have a complete history, physical examination, and serum TSH measurement. Although neck palpation is inexpensive, widely available, and simple to perform, it is notoriously imprecise and insensitive in the detection and estimation of size and number of thyroid nodules. All patients with a nodular thyroid should be evaluated with ultrasonography to confirm the presence of a mass, to assess whether the lesion is single or multiple, to determine whether it is of thyroidal origin, and to guide FNAB—the procedure of choice in the evaluation of thyroid nodules. Patients with multiple thyroid nodules have the same risk of malignancy as those with solitary thyroid nodules. Additional nodules are detected in up to 50% of patients presenting with a single palpable nodule when ultrasonography is performed. Selection of those nodules that require biopsy in patients with a multinodular goiter requires careful consideration. Certain ultrasonographic features are associated with increased risk of malignancy, including hypoechogenicity, the presence of microcalcifications, taller than wide shape, increased vascular flow, irregular borders, and absence of a halo. Nodules with any of these features should be aspirated preferentially. The combination of several of these sonographic features increases specificity.

The patient in this vignette has multiple benign-appearing nodules that are well defined, isoechoic, and have no suspicious ultrasonographic features. In this situation, the best recommendation is to aspirate the largest or dominant nodule(s) and to observe the rest with periodic ultrasonography (Answer A). Nodules that have been reported to have benign cytologic characteristics have a false-negative rate of up to 5%; thus, follow-up is recommended within 6 to 18 months of the initial investigation. Although there is no consensus on the definition of clinically significant growth of a thyroid nodule, guidelines from the American Thyroid Association recommend another biopsy when there has been at least a 50% increase in nodule volume within 6 to 18 months from the initial FNAB. If the nodule size is stable, another FNAB is not required and the interval before the next clinical evaluation may be extended to 3 to 5 years.

The American Thyroid Association guidelines provide nodule threshold sizes for FNAB. For mixed cystic-solid nodules such as the ones found in this patient, the recommendation is to aspirate those nodules larger than 2 cm. However, size is not a specific feature to predict risk of malignancy. Since 2 of the 3 mixed cystic-solid nodules in this patient are smaller than 2 cm and have no suspicious features on ultrasonography, performing FNAB on all 3 nodules (Answer B) is incorrect. Notably, the recent guidelines from the British Thyroid Association do not use nodule size as a criterion for FNAB in thyroid nodules. The American Thyroid Association guidelines are currently under revision.

Serum thyroglobulin can be elevated in most thyroid disease and its measurement is a nonsensitive and nonspecific test for thyroid cancer. Although the serum thyroglobulin concentration is useful to monitor patients for residual or recurrent disease after treatment for thyroid cancer, it is not recommended in the initial evaluation of thyroid nodules (thus, Answer C is incorrect). Thyroglobulin antibodies occur in approximately 25% of patients with thyroid cancer and in 10% of the general population.

When present, they may falsely lower serum thyroglobulin concentrations in immunometric assays. Thyroglobulin antibodies should be quantitatively assessed with every thyroglobulin measurement.

Although it is important to follow-up the aspirated and nonaspirated nodules with ultrasonography, it is advisable to aspirate the largest dominant nodule. Ultrasonographic follow-up without aspiration of the largest nodule (Answer D) i

items.statement: TSH >2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester may warrant levothyroxine in pregnancy.
items.explanation: ATA/Endocrine Society pregnancy TSH targets are lower than nonpregnant ranges.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "subclinical hypothyroidism in the first trimester"

items.statement: Long-term TSH suppression after thyroid cancer always remains necessary.
items.explanation: Low-risk survivors may relax suppression after sustained remission.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 13: "continued TSH suppression is no longer merited"

items.statement: Amiodarone can cause both thyrotoxicosis and hypothyroidism.
items.explanation: Iodine load and direct toxicity affect thyroid function.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 28: "amiodarone"

items.statement: Elevated T3 in critical illness always indicates thyroid storm.
items.explanation: Rarely elevated T3 in ICU should prompt hyperthyroidism evaluation, not automatic storm label without context.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "elevated T3 concentrations are highly unusual in critical illness"

items.statement: Benign FNAB cytology eliminates need for follow-up ultrasound.
items.explanation: Benign nodules still require serial imaging.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Thyroid nodules"

items.statement: Radioiodine is contraindicated in Graves ophthalmopathy without caution.
items.explanation: May worsen eye disease; steroids or alternative therapy considered.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 43: "Graves ophthalmopathy"

items.statement: Subclinical hypothyroidism in pregnancy should never be treated.
items.explanation: Treatment considered when TSH above trimester-specific range.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "levothyroxine replacement"

items.statement: Undetectable thyroglobulin on suppression supports remission in differentiated thyroid cancer.
items.explanation: Negative Tg and scan support low recurrence risk.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 13: "undetectable serum thyroglobulin"

items.assertion: Positive TPO antibodies in pregnancy always require immediate high-dose levothyroxine.
items.reason: TPO positivity alone without elevated TSH may be monitored; treatment depends on TSH.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "TPO antibodies"

items.assertion: Lowering levothyroxine in low-risk thyroid cancer survivors increases fracture and AF risk if still suppressed.
items.reason: Relaxing suppression reduces subclinical hyperthyroid complications.
items.explanation: Both true; suppression carries bone and AF risk.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 13: "detrimental effects of hyperthyroidism on bone density"

items.assertion: Type 1 amiodarone thyrotoxicosis is best treated with antithyroid drugs alone in all cases.
items.reason: Type 2 may need steroids; mixed types exist.
items.explanation: A oversimplified.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 28: "amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis"

items.assertion: All thyroid nodules >1 cm require surgery.
items.reason: FNAB and risk stratification guide management.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "FNAB"

items.assertion: Low T3 in ICU always requires levothyroxine.
items.reason: Sick euthyroid syndrome is adaptive; treatment not beneficial.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: Williams 15e ch48: "interventional studies have not shown benefit"

items.assertion: Methimazole is never used in pregnancy.
items.reason: Methimazole used in 2nd-3rd trimester; PTU preferred 1st trimester historically.
items.explanation: A false.

items.assertion: Thyroglobulin antibodies invalidate all Tg monitoring.
items.reason: Tg may still be tracked with caution; antibodies interfere but do not always preclude use.
items.explanation: A overstated.

items.assertion: Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication to diagnostic radioiodine scanning.
items.reason: RAI scanning contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal thyroid destruction risk.
